subject: AP Psychology
theme: Learning is a relatively permanent change in behavior or knowledge resulting from experience, occurring through classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and observational learning.
core_concepts: Classical conditioning (Pavlov) pairs a neutral stimulus with an unconditioned stimulus until the neutral stimulus alone triggers a conditioned response — the foundation of associative learning.

Key classical conditioning processes: acquisition (learning the association), extinction (weakening when US is removed), spontaneous recovery (reappearance after rest), generalization (responding to similar stimuli), and discrimination (responding only to the specific CS).
Operant conditioning (Skinner) shapes behavior through consequences: reinforcement increases behavior and punishment decreases it, each with positive (adding) and negative (removing) forms.

Reinforcement schedules — fixed-ratio, variable-ratio, fixed-interval, variable-interval — produce different patterns of responding; variable-ratio produces the highest, most consistent response rates.
Shaping uses successive approximations to reinforce behaviors that gradually resemble the target behavior, enabling learning of complex new behaviors.

Observational learning (Bandura) occurs by watching and imitating models; the Bobo doll experiment demonstrated that children learn aggressive behavior through observation without direct reinforcement.

Biological constraints on learning (Garcia, Seligman) show that organisms are predisposed to learn certain associations more easily than others — taste aversion learning and preparedness theory illustrate this.
Cognitive processes influence conditioning: latent learning (Tolman's cognitive maps) and insight learning (Köhler) show that learning can occur without observable behavior change or trial-and-error.

key_terms.definition: Learning in which a neutral stimulus becomes associated with an unconditioned stimulus to produce a conditioned response.
A stimulus that naturally and automatically triggers a response without prior learning (e.g., food causing salivation).
The unlearned, automatic response to the unconditioned stimulus (e.g., salivation to food).
A previously neutral stimulus that, after pairing with the US, triggers a conditioned response (e.g., bell after pairing with food).
The learned response to the conditioned stimulus (e.g., salivation to the bell alone).
The initial learning phase in which the association between CS and US is established through repeated pairings.
The weakening of a conditioned response when the CS is repeatedly presented without the US.
The reappearance of an extinguished conditioned response after a rest period.
The tendency to respond to stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus (e.g., fearing all dogs after being bitten by one).
The learned ability to distinguish between the conditioned stimulus and similar stimuli that do not signal the US.
Learning in which behavior is strengthened or weakened by its consequences (reinforcement or punishment).
Adding a pleasant stimulus after a behavior to increase the likelihood of that behavior recurring.
Removing an unpleasant stimulus after a behavior to increase the likelihood of that behavior recurring.
Adding an unpleasant stimulus after a behavior to decrease the likelihood of that behavior recurring.
Removing a pleasant stimulus after a behavior to decrease the likelihood of that behavior recurring.
Reinforcing successive approximations toward a desired behavior to teach complex new behaviors.
Reinforcement delivered after a set number of responses (e.g., paid per 10 items produced).
Reinforcement delivered after an unpredictable number of responses; produces high, steady response rates (e.g., slot machines).
Reinforcement delivered for the first response after a set time period (e.g., weekly paycheck).
Reinforcement delivered for the first response after an unpredictable time period (e.g., pop quizzes).
Learning by watching and imitating the behavior of others (models) without direct experience.
Learning that occurs without obvious reinforcement and is not demonstrated until an incentive is provided (Tolman's cognitive maps).
A learned avoidance of a food after a single pairing with nausea, even hours later — demonstrates biological preparedness.
The passive resignation that occurs when an organism learns it cannot control or escape aversive events (Seligman).

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests the distinction between positive/negative reinforcement and punishment — remember that 'positive' means adding a stimulus and 'negative' means removing one, while 'reinforcement' always increases behavior and 'punishment' always decreases it.
